State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance Executive legal Enforcement Conflict of Interest
(916) 322 5662 Administration Executive 322-6441 322 6444
322 5960

July 30, 1979

Betty Bryant, Director
Department of Economic and
Business Development
P.O. Box 1499
Sacramento, California 95805

Dear Mrs. Bryant:

Thank you for your letter of June 27, 1979 in which you requested
advice about the Department's Executive Loan Program. Specifically
you asked if executives loaned to the Department under the Executive
Loan Program are lobbyists as defined in Government Code Section 82039.

The Commission dealt with a similar question in an opinion
requested by Robert C. Herr, Attorney for Del Monte Corporation (3 FPPC
Opinions 11, No. 75-208, February 1, 1977) a copy of which is attached
for your information. In the Herr opinion, the Commission was asked
whether certain employees of the Del Monte Company were lobbyists
because they served on state agricultural boards and were paid by Del
Monte for their service on the boards. Relying upon Section 86300(a)
which exempts state employees from the lobbyist registration and report-
ing requirements of Government Code Section 86100 et. al. the Commission
concluded that "although the duties performed by board members may
involve activities that ordinarily would make them "lobbyists" within
the meaning of Government Code Section 82039, Del Monte employees are
exempt from the lobbyist reporting requirements by reason of Government
Code Section 86300(a) while functioning in their official capacities
as members of agricultural boards." However, the Commission also con-
cluded that members of the boards who attempt to influence legislative
action and who would be required to register as lobbyists except for
the provisions of Section 86300(a) may not make gifts aggregating more
than $10 in a calendar month to an elected state officer or legislative
official.

In the case you pose, the executives will function as employees of
the state although their salaries will continue to be paid by their
private employers. Thus, as was the case with the Del Monte employee -
agricultural board members, the executives loaned to the Department
will not have lobbyist registration reporting obligations while func-
tioning in their official capacities as employees of the Department of

Betty Bryant                    -2-                    July 30, 1979


Economic and Business Development. However, as is the case with the
Del Monte employees, executives on loan will be subject to the $10
per month limit on gifts to elected state officers and legislative
officials if they would qualify as legislative lobbyists were it not
for the provisions of section 86300(a).

You should also note that the Herr opinion concludes that Del
Monte may be required to report its salary payments to agricultural
board members as exchanges on its lobbyist employer reports or reports
filed pursuant to Government Code Section 86105(b). This same con-
clusion would be applicable to the firms that will loan executives
to your department. Thus, if any of the firms have an obligation to
report exchanges with officials in your department they will also be
required to report the salary of the loaned executives as an exchange.
See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18600, 18650.

If I can be of further assistance please contact me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [illegible signature]
                                        J. Richard Eichman
                                        Accounting Specialist

JRE:bw

STATE OF CALIFORNIA—BUSINESS AND TRANSPORTATION AGENCY                                    EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor

DEPARTMENT OF ECONOMIC AND BUSINESS DEVELOPMENT
1120 N STREET
P.O. BOX 1499
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95805

(916)322-1394


June 27, 1979


Mr. Thomas Houston
Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Mr. Houston:

The Department of Economic and Business Development, Business and Trans-
portation Agency, is operating an Executive Loan Program (ELP) under
which private corporations loan one or more of their executives to the
Department for a mutually agreed upon time period, normally running from
3 to 12 months.

The Department pays for expenses incurred by the loaned executive in the
course of conducting Department business, e.g., travel, per diem. The
"sponsoring" corporation continues the loaned executive on the company
payroll and pays any living expenses required by the loaned executive
while on assignment with the Department.

The purpose of the program is to expose private sector executives to the
workings of government, bring to government a private sector perspective,
and advise the Department on programs or policies affecting the business
climate in California. The purpose of this letter is to request your
opinion as to whether or not the participants in this program are lobbyists
as defined by Section 82039 of the Government Code.

Should you need additional information in order to respond to this request,
please call my assistant, Mr. Elliot Stevenson, at ATSS 492-3241.

Sincerely,


BETTY BRYANT
Director

cc: Alan Stein, Secretary, Business and Transportation Agency

State of California

Memorandum

To : Opinion Request Meeting Participants          Date : July 18, 1979


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Lee C. Rosenthal

Subject: Department of Business and Economic Development Loan Program

     The Department of Business and Economic Development has an
executive loan program whereby businesses loan employees to the
agency and also pay the employees salaries.  We dealt with a
similar situation in the Herr opinion (3 FPPC Opinions 11) and
concluded that Del Monte employees who served on agricultural
boards and were paid by Del Monte for their service did not become lobbyists.  The Herr opinion concludes that the board
members are state "employees" for purposes of the Section 86300(a)
lobbyist reporting exemption for state employees.

     The Department of Business situation seems analogous to
the Herr opinion.  However, that opinion contains some analysis
that could lend on to differentiate it from the present case.
Part of the rationale for the opinion's conclusion is that the
applicable statutes require agricultural board members to serve
the interests of the industry and therefore the employer.  Thus,
when a board member's "lobbying" is to the benefit of his employer,
he is only doing what the statute under which his board is
created commands.  In the case of the executive loan program,
the public role of the privately paid employee may be inimical
to the interests of his employer.

     The Herr opinion also relies on the fact that the agricultural
board member will have to disclose his financial interests and
disqualify himself in some instances.  I would think that the
same would hold true for persons in the executive loan program,
but I am not sure.  Are these people "employees" or "consultants"
for purposes of disqualification and disclosure?

     Attached are the relevant pages from the Herr opinion.

LR:plh
Attachment

October 26, 1978


C. Thomas Apple
Department of Economic and
Business Development
P.O. Box 1499
Sacramento, California 95805

Dear Mr. Apple:

This letter will confirm the advice given you regarding
the reporting obligation of IBM Corporation (IBM) under the
Political Reform Act of 1974. You asked how IBM should
report the salary and reimbursed extraordinary living expenses
of an executive loaned to the State of California.

In an opinion requested by John C. Morrissey, FPPC
Opinion 130 (75-066, October 1, 1975), the Commission held
that salary payments to company employees who are also
agency officials are exchanges, not payments to influence
administrative action, and that such payments may be reportable if the salary exceeds $1,000 in a calendar year. Routine
fringe benefits are not part of the "exchange" for the purposes
of determining whether the $1,000 threshold is met.

IBM must only report exchanges: (1) with agency officials
of agencies which are, or should be, listed on its lobbying
registration statement; or (2) if IBM spends $250 or more in
any one month to influence legislative or administrative action,
and an agency official with whom it engages in such an exchange
is an official of an agency whose administrative action IBM
attempts to influence.

C. Thomas Apple                    -2-                    October 26, 1978

     Accordingly, IBM must report the payments for salary
and extraordinary living expenses made to you as exchanges
if the exchanges exceed $1,000 (86109(d)).

     If I can be of further assistance please contact me.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        J. Richard Eichman
                                        Compliance Representative

cc: Ed Shirball
    IBM Corporation
    520 Capitol Mall
    Sacramento, CA 95814

more carefully.

BUSINESS AND TRANSLATION AGENCY                                EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor

DEPARTMENT OF ECONOMIC AND BUSINESS DEVELOPMENT

1122 N STREET
P.O. BOX 1499
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95805

(916) 322-1394

TO:  [illegible]
     YOUR | INFO. | COMMENTS | FILE                           June 19, 1978

Mr. Clarence E. Frizzell                                       RECD JUN 20 1978
Vice President Manufacturing
IBM Corporation                        PLS | HANDLE | RETURN | DISCUSS
5600 Cottle Road                                              WITH ME
San Jose, CA  95193

Dear Mr. Frizzell:

Last year the State of California established a pilot program in
which executives from private corporations were loaned to work
in state government. To date two corporations have participated,
Chevron USA and Pacific Telephone.

I am writing to you to find out if IBM might have an interest in
sponsoring an employee for a high level assignment in state govern-
ment for six months to a year.

The main reasons for the program are to bring people with business
perspectives into state government and to help improve communica-
tions between the public and private sectors.

Our experience with the Executive Loan Program has been very positive.
We feel it has had value to both our Department and to the sponsoring
companies. The program also provides the executive with an extra-
ordinary developmental opportunity in which he or she can gain a
unique insight into the operation of state government.

We are currently looking for a candidate for an assignment as
Assistant to the Director of the Department of Economic and Business
Development. A more detailed description of the Executive Loan
Program and a specific job description are attached. We would like
the assignment to start as soon as possible.

I would be happy to discuss this program with you further on the
telephone if you have any questions. We would appreciate an indica-
tion of whether your company would have an interest in pursuing
this further.

I look forward to hearing from you shortly.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        BETTY BRYANT
                                        Director

Attachment
CC: Alan Stein, R.T. Silverman

Executive Loan Program
Department of Economic and Business Development


The Department of Economic and Business Development

In January 1978, the Department of Economic and Business Development
was established to provide an organization through which communication
could be enhanced between state government and the private sector. The
goal is to create and maintain an environment in California in which
the private sector can develop and expand to meet the expanding job
needs of the state's residents.

The department has six offices:

1. The Office of Business and Industrial Development works with
   businesses that are considering moving to California, busi-
   nesses that are considering expansions in California and
   businesses that are having difficulties operating in Califor-
   nia due to certain laws and regulations.

2. The Office of International Trade provides tools and services
   that assist California manufacturing, agricultural and
   technical service businesses expand their sales through
   export markets.

3. The Office of Economic Planning, Policy and Research develops
   and analyzes data on the state's economy. It also provides
   analysis for policy guidance on the economic impact of new
   and existing state laws and regulations.

4. The Office of Visitor Services acts as a pivotal point for
   the expansion of the state's tourism industry.

5. The Office of Small Business Development addresses the unique
   financial and management assistance needs of small businesses.

6. The Office of Local Economic Development assists local
   communities with economic development planning.

Executive Loan Program

The Executive Loan Program was developed to bring a business pro-
spective into state government and to enhance the communication process
between the public and private sectors both now and in the future.

Under the program executives from the private sector are loaned to the
state for a period of six months to one year. During that time they
work as a full-time state employee and are involved in a variety of
activities: including research, analysis and administrative decision-
making. Normal activities will bring loaned executives in contact

with a wide range of individuals working in both the public and
private sectors.

The Executive Loan Program was initiated in 1977 in the Office of
Business Development, the predecessor of the Department of Economic
and Business Development. Our experience with the program over the
short time it has been in existence has been quite favorable.

Executive Loan Program Terms

The services of the loaned executive under the program are considered
a community service donation. The executive's salary and extraordinary
living expenses are paid by the sponsoring company or trade association.
Any expenses incurred by the executive in the performance of his job
with the state are reimbursed by the state according to state personnel
policies.

Conflicts of Interest

While the executive is on loan to the state his job activities should
solely be related to state business. It is important for the sponsor
to remember that the success of the program is dependent on the
executive's autonomy from his or her company in the performance of
his state duties.

It is also important for the executive to avoid becoming involved in
sensitive areas that either directly affect or have an appearance of
affecting his or her sponsoring company or trade association.

Reporting Under the Fair Political Practices Act

The sponsoring company or trade association should report the loaned
executive's salary and reimbursed extraordinary living expenses in
the monthly report to the Fair Political Practices Commission as an
independent item. It should not be included as a lobbying expense
since the loaned executive will not be working in an advocate capacity.

Job Description

Assistant to the Director
Department of Economic and Business Development


Job Responsibilities

Perform project oriented work for the Department in general and
for one or more of the Offices within the Department. These projects
might include:

- Assist companies considering locating in California.

- Assist companies having specific problems with California
laws and regulations.

- Analyzing legislation that impacts the business climate in
California.

- Assist in the development of a legislative program that will
favorably effect economic development in California.

- Develop and assist with programming that will facilitate
California trade expansion.

- Speak at seminars, conferences, and banquets to help
inform the public of the Department's activities.

- Other special projects as determined by the Director.

The performance of projects in the Department of Economic and
Business Development is likely to bring the loaned executive into
contact with many state departments, boards and commissions, state
legislators and legislative staffs, federal agencies, consular staffs
of foreign business leaders, and domestic and foreign press.

Job Term

From experience, a term of 6 months to 1 year is the most advantageous
period for this position. A shorter term can be arranged if specific
circumstances warrant.

Qualifications

Minimum of 8 years of broad business background. Previous experience
in government or government relations is not a prerequisite and is
in fact discouraged. Experience in the following areas may be helpful:

Business Planning
Plant Operations
Plant Siting Analysis
Economic Analysis
Finance

The leaned executive must be able to react to a number of diverse
and complex situations. With little or no direction and
direction will be provided, therefore the individual must be
aggressive, outgoing, and self-starting. The leaned executive
should be willing to put in long hours and travel throughout
the state as the job requires.

Chevron U.S.A. Inc.
555 Market Street, San Francisco, CA 94105
Mail Address: P.O. Box 3045, San Francisco, CA 94119

July 13, 1978

Richard V. Kreuzten
Assistant Manager
Government Affairs

Ms. Betty Bryant
Department of Economics and
Business Development
Director
1120 N Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Betty:

Attached is a letter supporting the loaned Executive
Program from the president of Standard Oil of California
to the Policy Committee of the California Roundtable.
Knowing what kind of support is being given the program
and where it is being directed may be helpful to you in
your recruiting efforts. Many of the presidents listed
on the letterhead are people to which inquiries from
the Department of Economics and Business Development
were directed.

I am continuing to develop other ways through which we
can support your recruitment efforts from this end.

Sincerely,

W. B. May

WBM:mt

Attachment

cc: Mr. J. F. Mayer

San Francisco, CA
May 31, 1978

EXECUTIVE LOAN PROGRAM -
STATE OF CALIFORNIA

MEMBERS, POLICY COMMITTEE
THE CALIFORNIA ROUNDTABLE

For the past six months our company has loaned one of our
executives to the State of California Department of Economic
and Business Development. The executive has worked full-
time as a State employee on a variety of projects including
business analysis, research, and planning. His salary and
living expenses have been paid by us, and the State has
picked up job-related expenditures.

The program has been very successful from both our standpoint
and the State's. In both cases the participating firms, because
they are so pleased with the results, the Department of
Economic and Business Development has asked for our help
in extending the program to other companies. I am writing
you because I believe the California Roundtable membership
includes most of the other companies who might wish to
participate.

The program has provided an excellent opportunity to develop
and broaden the perspective of our executive on state
government, and has increased his understanding of the
operations and processes within the state system. It has
also increased the understanding of state officials of
business problems, and their appreciation of the ability
and integrity of people from the business community.

For these reasons, I believe it would be appropriate for
the California Roundtable to promote the program among
its members. It should be stressed that the quality and
impartiality of the individual selected are absolute
requirements if the program is to continue to be favorably
accepted. A description of the Executive Loan Program
and a more detailed job description for the executive
that participates are attached.

- 2 -

I would be glad to discuss this briefly at the meeting on
June 7, and to forward the names of any CCTA members
who wish to participate to Mr. Alan Stein, Secretary of
Business and Transportation. If the Committee concurs,
I would also be glad to write to other California
Roundtable member companies and invite them to correspond
directly with Mr. Stein if they are interested.


J. R. GREY

Attachments

sections.question: Specifically you asked if executives loaned to the Department under the Executive Loan Program are lobbyists as defined in Government Code Section 82039.
sections.conclusion: The executives loaned to the Department will not have lobbyist registration reporting obligations while functioning in their official capacities as employees of the Department of Economic and Business Development. However, executives on loan will be subject to the $10 per month limit on gifts to elected state officers and legislative officials if they would qualify as legislative lobbyists were it not for the provisions of Section 86300(a). Firms loaning executives may be required to report salary payments to loaned executives as exchanges on their lobbyist employer reports.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether executives loaned to the Department of Economic and Business Development under the Executive Loan Program, who are paid by their private employers but function as state employees, qualify as lobbyists under Government Code Section 82039.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Loaned executives are exempt from lobbyist registration requirements under Government Code Section 86300(a) while functioning as state employees, but remain subject to the $10 per month gift limit to state officials. Sponsoring corporations may need to report executive salaries as exchanges if they have lobbyist reporting obligations.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple related letters and memoranda. Primary FPPC response dated July 30, 1979 from J. Richard Eichman references similar Herr opinion (75-208). Supporting documents include initial request from Betty Bryant (June 27, 1979), internal FPPC memorandum (July 18, 1979), prior IBM letter (October 26, 1978), and recruitment correspondence. No explicit 'CONCLUSION' header but clear advisory conclusion provided in response letter.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Specifically you asked if executives loaned to the Department under the Executive Loan Program are lobbyists as defined in Government Code Section 82039.

CONCLUSION: The executives loaned to the Department will not have lobbyist registration reporting obligations while functioning in their official capacities as employees of the Department of Economic and Business Development. However, executives on loan will be subject to the $10 per month limit on gifts to elected state officers and legislative officials if they would qualify as legislative lobbyists were it not for the provisions of Section 86300(a). Firms loaning executives may be required to report salary payments to loaned executives as exchanges on their lobbyist employer reports.
embedding.first_500_words: July 30, 1979 Betty Bryant, Director Department of Economic and Business Development P.O. Box 1499 Sacramento, California 95805 Dear Mrs. Bryant: Thank you for your letter of June 27, 1979 in which you requested advice about the Department's Executive Loan Program. Specifically you asked if executives loaned to the Department under the Executive Loan Program are lobbyists as defined in Government Code Section 82039. The Commission dealt with a similar question in an opinion requested by Robert C. Herr, Attorney for Del Monte Corporation (3 FPPC Opinions ll, No. 75-208, February 1, 1977) a copy of which is attached for your information. In the Herr opinion, the Commission was asked whether certain employees of the Del Monte Company were lobbyists because they served on state agricultural boards and were paid by Del Monte for their service on the boards. Relying upon Section 86300(a) which exempts state employees from the lobbyist registration and reporting requirements of Government Code Section 86100 et. al, the Commission concluded that "although the duties performed by board members may involve activities that ordinarily would make them 'lobbyists' within the meaning of Government Code Section 82039, Del Monte employees are exempt from the lobbyist reporting requirements by reason of Government Code Section 86300(a) while functioning in their official capacities as members of agricultural boards." However, the Commission also concluded that members of the boards who attempt to influence legislative action and who would be required to register as lobbyists except for the provisions of Section 86300(a) may not make gifts aggregating more than $10 in a calendar month to an elected state officer or legislative official. In the case you pose, the executives will function as employees of the state although their salaries will continue to be paid by their private employers. Thus, as was the case with the Del Monte employee - agricultural board members, the executives loaned to the Department will not have lobbyist registration reporting obligations while functioning in their official capacities as employees of the Department of Economic and Business Development. However, as is the case with the Del Monte employees, executives on loan will be subject to the $10 per month limit on gifts to elected state officers and legislative officials if they would qualify as legislative lobbyists were it not for the provisions of Section 86300(a). You should also note that the Herr opinion concludes that Del Monte may be required to report its salary payments to agricultural board members as exchanges on its lobbyist employer reports or reports filed pursuant to Government Code Section 86108(b). This same conclusion would be applicable to the firms that will loan executives to your department. Thus, if any of the firms have an obligation to report exchanges with officials in your department they will also be required to report the salary of the loaned executives as an exchange. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18600, 18650. If I can be of further assistance please contact me. Sincerely, J. Richard Eichman Accounting Specialist JRE:bw June
embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that executives loaned to the Department of Economic and Business Development under an executive loan program are not required to register as lobbyists while functioning as state employees, relying on precedent from the Herr opinion regarding Del Monte employees serving on agricultural boards. However, such executives are subject to gift restrictions and their sponsoring corporations may have exchange reporting obligations.
